perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall keep the Confidential Materials confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Materials in any manner whatsoever, provided, however, that (i) Buyer may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Materials to which Owner gives its prior written consent; and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Materials may be disclosed to Buyer’s Representatives who need to know that information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction with Owner and who agree to keep that information confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall endeavor to keep the Confidential Materials confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Materials in any manner whatsoever, provided, however, that (i) Buyer may make reasonable disclosures of information contained in the Confidential Materials; and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Materials may be disclosed to Buyer’s Representatives who seem to need that information for the purpose of vaguely evaluating a Transaction with Owner.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity. 'Endeavor to keep' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. 'Reasonable disclosures' is vague and subjective. 'Seem to need' and 'vaguely evaluating' introduce further uncertainty regarding the criteria for disclosing information to representatives and the purpose of the evaluation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text uses terms like 'endeavor to keep' and 'reasonable disclosures', weakening the standard of care expected of the buyer, and thus potentially failing to meet the reasonable efforts standard for protecting trade secrets under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the buyer's obligation to protect confidential materials, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection. The original text stated that 'Buyer shall keep the Confidential Materials confidential', where as the changed text says 'Buyer shall endeavor to keep the Confidential Materials confidential'. Changing the disclosure term from 'may make any disclosure' to 'may make reasonable disclosures' also weakens the language. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites 'California Code, CIV 3426' as the 'Uniform Trade Secrets Act'. The 'endeavor to keep' and 'reasonable disclosures' clauses introduce subjectivity and uncertainty, making it more difficult to prove that the buyer made reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, thus contradicting the requirement for reasonable efforts implied by the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall at all times conduct its due diligence review, inspections and examinations in a manner so as to not cause damage, loss, cost or expense to Owner, the Property or the tenants of the Property and so as to not interfere with or disturb any tenant at the Property, and Buyer will indemnify, protect, defend, and hold Owner and the Property harmless from and against any such breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall attempt to conduct its due diligence review, inspections and examinations in a manner that minimizes damage, loss, cost or expense to Owner, the Property or the tenants of the Property and so as to reasonably avoid interfering with or disturbing any tenant at the Property, and Buyer will consider indemnifying, protecting, defending, and holding Owner and the Property harmless from and against any such breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity and weaken the buyer's obligations. Replacing 'shall' with 'shall attempt' and 'so as to not' with 'minimizes' and 'reasonably avoid' reduces the strictness of the requirement. The alteration of 'will indemnify' to 'will consider indemnifying' creates uncertainty about whether the buyer is obligated to provide indemnity. These changes significantly weaken the protection for the Owner.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714 establishes the principle that everyone is responsible for injury caused to another by their want of ordinary care or skill in the management of their property or person. By changing the obligation to avoid causing damage or disturbance to a weaker 'attempt to minimize' or 'reasonably avoid', the buyer's duty of care is lessened. This could potentially contradict the standard of ordinary care required under California law if the buyer's actions during due diligence result in foreseeable harm that could have been avoided with reasonable care.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the buyer's obligation to avoid causing harm during due diligence, potentially contradicting the duty of ordinary care required by California Civil Code § 1714. The original text states the buyer 'shall at all times conduct' due diligence 'so as to not cause damage', whereas the changed text uses 'shall attempt to conduct' in a manner that 'minimizes damage' and 'reasonably avoid interfering'. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the buyer's accountability. The alteration from 'will indemnify' to 'will consider indemnifying' further weakens the protection for the owner, creating uncertainty about indemnity. The scraped law snippet CIV 1714(a) states 'Everyone is responsible...for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill'. The changes weaken the original obligation, creating a potential situation where the buyer's 'attempt to minimize' may not meet the 'ordinary care' standard if damage occurs, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall reimburse Owner for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorney’s fees, incurred by Owner if Owner successfully enforces the obligations of Buyer and its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer might reimburse Owner for some costs and expenses, possibly including attorney’s fees, if Owner arguably enforces some obligations of Buyer and its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The language has been weakened significantly. 'Shall reimburse' is replaced with 'might reimburse,' making reimbursement discretionary. 'All costs and expenses' is reduced to 'some costs and expenses,' and 'reasonable attorney's fees' becomes 'possibly including attorney's fees,' both introducing uncertainty about the extent of reimbursement. 'Successfully enforces' is changed to 'arguably enforces,' making the trigger for reimbursement vague and subject to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally allows parties to contract for attorney's fees. While it doesn't mandate attorney fee provisions, it anticipates that contracts specifying such fees will be enforced. The modified language uses 'might reimburse,' making the awarding of attorney fees at the discretion of the Buyer and hence is contradictory. It creates ambiguity about the recoverability of attorney's fees, which could hinder the Owner's ability to enforce the agreement due to the uncertainty of recovering legal costs, undermining the purpose of § 1021.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates reimbursement, while the changed text makes it discretionary. CCP 1021 states that the compensation of attorneys is left to the agreement of the parties. The changed text introduces significant ambiguity regarding the obligation to reimburse attorney's fees, potentially undermining the original intent and enforceability of the attorney's fees provision. By changing 'shall reimburse' to 'might reimburse' and introducing uncertainty regarding the costs and the enforcement standard, the modified text weakens the agreement, thus contradicting the anticipated enforcement outlined in CCP 1021.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer and Buyer’s Broker agree to indemnify and hold harmless TKG and Owner, their respective affiliates, partners, subsidiaries, reasonable attorney’s fees arising out of any claim or claims by any broker, finder or similar agent (including but not limited to Broker) for commissions, fees or other 3288 Steiner Street, Suite 201, San Francisco, CA 94123 EMAIL: jgates@thekasegroup.com | TEL: 415.231.5231 | FAX: 925.406.0813 | www.thekasegroup.com compensation for bringing about any sale of the Property.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer and Buyer’s Broker may consider indemnifying and holding harmless TKG and Owner, their respective affiliates, partners, subsidiaries, for potentially reasonable attorney’s fees vaguely connected to any claim or claims by any broker, finder or similar agent for commissions, fees or other compensation for possibly assisting in the sale of the Property.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the indemnification obligation significantly. 'Agree to indemnify and hold harmless' becomes 'may consider indemnifying and holding harmless,' making it discretionary. 'Reasonable attorney's fees' is modified to 'potentially reasonable attorney's fees vaguely connected to', increasing ambiguity and reducing the scope of potential coverage. The addition of 'possibly assisting' creates an opportunity to dispute the connection between the broker's actions and the sale, further weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 provides rules for interpreting indemnity agreements, assuming a clear intention to indemnify. The modified clause uses weaker, ambiguous language ('may consider,' 'potentially reasonable,' 'vaguely connected,' 'possibly assisting'), making the scope of indemnification unclear. This introduces uncertainty about the indemnitee's rights and protections, potentially conflicting with the interpretive framework established by California Civil Code § 2778, which expects a clearer expression of intent to indemnify.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2778. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the indemnification obligation, creating a contradiction. The original text states the buyer and broker 'agree to indemnify and hold harmless,' while the changed text uses 'may consider indemnifying and holding harmless'. This introduces ambiguity and discretion where there was previously a clear obligation. Furthermore, modifying 'reasonable attorney's fees' to 'potentially reasonable attorney's fees vaguely connected to,' and 'possibly assisting' further erode the certainty and scope of the indemnity. Per California Civil Code § 2778, 'In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears.' The changes introduce ambiguity, which contradicts the expectation of a clear expression of intent to indemnify, making the contract ambiguous and difficult to enforce in alignment with CA Civil Code 2778.
